headlines.geopolitics: Mike Johnson warns Sharia law 'serious problem' after GOP rep's anti-Muslim post
headlines.economy: Culture at Columbia Shielded Sexual Assault by Physician, Report Finds
headlines.domestic: Fox News Poll: Voters expect AI to transform our lives — but today is not that day

metadata.daily_do: Check one piece of content you shared today to see if it was AI-generated. Just one. Most of us can't tell as well as we think we can.

clips.caption: When Duke tries to explain assimilation and it goes sideways
Pringle drops truth bombs about moral panics
Jax discovers the most Florida headline ever

script.text: Look, I'm not saying we should ban anything, but when someone moves here and refuses to learn that bacon cheeseburgers are a constitutional right, we've got a problem.
Good morning, I'm Leo Barros and this is The Friction. It's Monday, March 10th, 2026. Let's get you caught up.

Three stories driving the conversation today. First, geopolitics — House Speaker Mike Johnson is defending anti-Muslim comments from Tennessee Republican Andy Ogles by warning that Sharia law poses a quote 'serious problem' in America.
Oh, here we go.

Johnson says Sharia law conflicts with the Constitution while Democrats are calling the rhetoric racist and bigoted. Conservative Republicans are now forming something called a 'Sharia-free America Caucus.'
A caucus? They're forming a whole caucus? What's next, the 'No Pineapple on Pizza Caucus?'
I'd join that one.

Second, the economy — An external report found that Columbia University and NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital administrators mishandled sexual assault allegations against physician Robert Hadden. Two doctors have stepped down following the findings.
Institutional protection of predators. Tale as old as time.

And domestic news — A new Fox poll shows 87% of voters expect AI to significantly change American life, but only 47% find it useful right now. There's a massive generational divide, and 89% want clear disclosure when content is AI-generated.
Only 47% find it useful? What are the other 53% doing, asking ChatGPT to help them open pickle jars?
I asked ChatGPT to write my Tinder bio. Still single, so the AI revolution might be overhyped.
Alright, let's dig into this Sharia law story because it's hitting all the culture war buttons. Duke, you want to start us off here?
Look, I think Johnson's point about assimilation got lost in the noise. When people immigrate here, there's an expectation that you adapt to American legal systems, American values.
But that's not what Andy Ogles said. He said Muslims don't belong in American society. Period. That's not about assimilation, that's about religious bigotry.
I'm not defending Ogles' language. I'm saying there's a legitimate conversation about— look, if someone comes here and wants to implement a legal system that conflicts with the Constitution—
But they can't! That's the whole point! The Constitution already prevents that! This is manufactured fear-mongering about a problem that doesn't exist.
Hold on, let me get the facts straight here. Sharia law cannot be implemented at any governmental level in the US because of constitutional protections. That's established law.
Right, but there are communities where people try to handle disputes through religious arbitration instead of going through the courts.
Which is legal! Orthodox Jewish communities do the same thing with Beth Din courts. Catholics have canonical courts. This is how religious freedom works in America.
Wait, so we're forming a congressional caucus to solve a problem that the Constitution already solved 235 years ago? That's like forming a caucus against gravity.
Look, I'm not saying we should ban anything, but when someone moves here and refuses to learn that bacon cheeseburgers are a constitutional right, we've got a problem.
Did you just— bacon cheeseburgers are not in the Constitution, Duke.
Life, liberty, and the pursuit of deliciousness. I'm pretty sure that's in there.
Can we— can we get back to the actual issue here? Bree, what's your take on how this rhetoric affects Muslim Americans?

This is exactly what happens when politicians use fear to score points. You have Muslim Americans who've been here for generations, who serve in our military, who are teachers and doctors and engineers, and suddenly they're being told they don't belong.
But nobody's saying that about Muslims who've assimilated. The issue is with people who come here and—
Who defines assimilation? Because it sounds like you're saying Muslims have to give up their religious practices to be real Americans.
That's not what I'm saying at all. Practice your religion. Go to mosque. Celebrate your holidays. But don't try to impose religious law on other people.
But they're not! Show me one example of Muslims successfully imposing Sharia law on non-Muslims in America. One example.
I mean, there have been cases where—
Where what? Where Muslim families handled a dispute privately? That's not imposing law on anyone.
This feels like when my mom was convinced that Pokémon cards were satanic. Like, show me the demon, Mom. Show me the actual demon.
The polling on this is interesting though. There clearly is public sentiment that Johnson is tapping into, whether it's based in reality or not.
Right, because politicians keep stoking these fears! It's a feedback loop. Create the panic, then point to the panic as justification for the rhetoric.
Or maybe people have legitimate concerns about maintaining American legal traditions and politicians are responding to that.
American legal traditions like religious freedom? Like equal protection under the law? Those traditions?
Touché. But you know what I mean.
I think what Duke means is there's a difference between private religious practice and trying to supersede civil law, which as we've established, can't happen anyway.
So we're forming a congressional caucus to prevent something that's already prevented by the document they all swore to protect. This is peak government efficiency right here.
Professor, you mentioned the Red Scare earlier. Are we seeing something similar here?
Actually, Leo, everyone assumes this is like the 1920s Red Scare, but it's not. That comparison misses the crucial difference.

The Red Scare was about a real geopolitical threat. The Soviet Union existed. They had nuclear weapons. They were actively trying to infiltrate American institutions. The fear was outsized, but the threat was real.
So what's different here?

This is closer to the Anti-Masonic Party of the 1830s. They were convinced that Freemasons were secretly controlling the government. The Masons were a real organization, they had rituals, they had influence. But the conspiracy was entirely manufactured.
Wait, there was an actual Anti-Masonic political party?
Oh yes. They held the first national party convention in American history in 1831. They nominated William Wirt for president. He got seven electoral votes.
Seven electoral votes for being afraid of guys in funny hats doing secret handshakes. Democracy is wild.
The pattern is identical. Take a visible minority group, attribute secret motives to them, claim they're undermining American institutions, then build political power by promising to stop them.

The Anti-Masonic Party eventually collapsed because people realized the Masons weren't actually running a shadow government. They were just middle-aged men who liked elaborate ceremonies and charity work.
So the moral panic burns itself out when reality doesn't match the hysteria?
Usually. But not before it damages innocent people and wastes considerable political energy. The Anti-Masonic Party lasted eight years. That's eight years of distraction from actual governance.
And you think that's what we're seeing with the Sharia-free caucus?
I think we're seeing politicians manufacture a crisis to appeal to voters who are genuinely anxious about cultural change. The anxiety is real. The threat is not.
But cultural change can be threatening. People want to preserve what they value about their communities.
Absolutely. But the question is whether you preserve culture through exclusion or through confident integration. The Anti-Masons thought exclusion was the answer. History suggests otherwise.
Alright, it's time for Rapid Fire, where we talk about the news stories that make you go 'wait, what?' First up: Palantir's AI weaponry has been deployed to find office chairs for government staff.
I'm sorry, what?
Yeah, the same AI that's supposed to track terrorists is now optimizing furniture procurement. Apparently it can identify the perfect ergonomic chair for a GS-12 analyst in seventeen seconds.
I mean, have you seen government office chairs? This might actually be a national security issue.
This is what we're using AI for? Office furniture?
Next story: Louisiana classrooms are required to post the Ten Commandments by this Friday. The deadline is Friday, and apparently half the schools are scrambling to find poster board.
They're gonna end up with the Ten Commandments printed on computer paper in Comic Sans font.
Thou shalt not kill, but in Helvetica.
Oh, this next one is peak Florida. A drug dealer locked his customer in a storage unit for months over unpaid debt.
Months? How did— never mind, I don't want to know.
The customer was found by storage facility employees when they heard yelling during an auction. He'd been living off of energy drinks and beef jerky that other people stored in the unit.
That's not a kidnapping, that's just really aggressive customer service.
This is horrible! That's false imprisonment! This person could have died!
I know, I know, but also— the customer reviews for this dealer must be incredible. 'Great product, terrible late payment policy. Two stars.'
Moving on before Bree has an aneurysm.
Last one: A candidate named 'Literally Anybody Else' officially appears on the Texas mayoral ballot. That's their legal name now.
You know what? I respect the honesty.
In ancient Rome, citizens sometimes voted for 'None of the Above' by writing in the name of a dead emperor. Same energy.
Their campaign slogan is 'Why not?' and their platform is 'I literally cannot be worse than the current options.'
This is what voter disillusionment looks like when it gets creative.
According to early polling, Literally Anybody Else is in third place with 18% of the vote.
Democracy is beautiful, folks.
Alright, time for the Daily Do. Today's action item comes from that AI poll we talked about earlier.
89% of people want clear disclosure when content is AI-generated, but only 60% think they can actually tell when something is AI-created. That gap should worry all of us.
So here's what you can do today: Check one piece of content you shared today to see if it was AI-generated. Just one. A meme, a news article, a video, whatever.

Look for telltale signs. Weird hands in images, too-perfect writing, facts that don't check out. Most of us can't tell as well as we think we can, and that's a problem when we're sharing information with people we care about.
This is actually really important. Misinformation spreads fastest through trusted networks. Your family believes stuff you share because they trust you.
I checked my last Instagram story and it turns out I shared a picture of a cat that doesn't exist. The cat looked really happy though.
Jenny showed me a video last week that I swore was real. Turns out it was completely generated. Looked perfect to me.
The technology is getting better faster than our ability to detect it. So just check one thing today. Be skeptical of perfect content. Trust but verify.
As Mark Twain never actually said, 'A lie can travel halfway around the world while the truth is putting on its shoes.' He didn't say it, but somebody did, and now it's on the internet forever.
Perfect example right there. Thanks for listening to The Friction. We're the show that argues so you don't have to. See you tomorrow.
And remember, literally anybody else could be running this show, but you're stuck with us.
I see what you did there. Nice callback, old man.
